does: This paper develops a multidimensional evaluation framework for reward models by introducing MRMBench, a benchmark with six probing tasks targeting distinct preference dimensions like helpfulness, harmlessness, and honesty. Rather than assessing reward models solely on aggregate pairwise ranking accuracy, the method decomposes performance across individual preference aspects. The authors additionally propose inference-time probing, a technique that identifies which preference dimensions a reward model actually uses during prediction, improving interpretability. Experiments demonstrate that MRMBench scores correlate strongly with downstream alignment performance of large language models, establishing it as a predictive evaluation metric for reward model quality.
connects: This work builds on the long-standing challenge of evaluating alignment and reward learning systems, extending beyond simple pairwise comparison metrics that dominated earlier reward model assessment. It responds to limitations in prior evaluation approaches that treat preference learning as a black box, drawing on emerging research recognizing that reward models must capture multifaceted human preferences rather than singular objectives. The probing methodology connects to interpretability research in machine learning that dissects learned representations to understand model behavior, and relates to work on compositional preference learning and multi-objective alignment in LLMs. The benchmark construction follows best practices from established ML evaluation frameworks.
enables: This framework enables more targeted development of reward models by identifying specific preference dimensions where current methods fail, guiding future architectural and training improvements. The interpretability analysis via inference-time probing opens pathways for better understanding and debugging reward model failures, potentially enabling techniques that explicitly enforce balanced preference learning. The strong correlation with downstream LLM alignment performance suggests MRMBench could become a standard reference metric for the field, reducing reliance on expensive full-model alignment evaluations. Future work could extend the framework to additional preference dimensions, explore how probing scales to larger models, and investigate whether multidimensional optimization during reward model training improves overall alignment outcomes.
